title: Pointers and references to the base class of derived objects

exercises.title: Base reference calls Base version
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Demonstrate that calling a member function through a `Base&` reference invokes the base-class version, even when the actual object is a derived type.

## Input

- A single line containing a string `name` (1-50 characters, no spaces).

## Output

Exactly three lines:
1. `Direct: <name> says Woof`
2. `Via ref: <name> says ...`
3. `Via ptr: <name> says ...`

where `<name>` is the name read from input.

## Requirements

- Define a class `Animal` with a `std::string` member `m_name`, a constructor taking `const std::string&`, a public `const std::string& getName() const` accessor, and a public `std::string_view speak() const` that returns `"..."`.
- Define a class `Dog` that publicly inherits from `Animal`. `Dog`'s constructor takes `const std::string&` and forwards it to `Animal`. `Dog` has its own `std::string_view speak() const` that returns `"Woof"`.
- In `main`, read the name and create a `Dog` object.
- Print `Direct: <name> says <speak>` by calling `speak()` directly on the `Dog` object.
- Create a `const Animal&` reference to the `Dog` and print `Via ref: <name> says <speak>` using the reference.
- Create a `const Animal*` pointer to the `Dog` and print `Via ptr: <name> says <speak>` using the pointer.
- The reference and pointer calls must demonstrate that the `Animal` version of `speak()` is invoked.

test_cases.label: Dog named Rex
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Direct: Rex says Woof
Via ref: Rex says ...
Via ptr: Rex says ...

test_cases.label: Dog named Buddy
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Direct: Buddy says Woof
Via ref: Buddy says ...
Via ptr: Buddy says ...

test_cases.label: Dog named Max
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Direct: Max says Woof
Via ref: Max says ...
Via ptr: Max says ...

exercises.title: Function taking Base& hides derived behavior
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Write a `report` function that accepts a `const Shape&` and prints the shape's description. Show that when a `Circle` is passed, the base `Shape` description is printed instead of the `Circle` description, because the function parameter is a base reference.

## Input

- Line 1: a string `shapeName` (1-50 characters, no spaces).
- Line 2: a double `radius` (0.1 to 1000.0).

## Output

Exactly three lines:
1. `Circle: <shapeName> r=<radius>` (direct call on the Circle)
2. `Report: <shapeName>: generic shape` (via the report function taking const Shape&)
3. `Direct: <shapeName> r=<radius>` (direct call on Circle again, proving the object itself is fine)

The radius is printed with default `std::cout` formatting (no fixed precision).

## Requirements

- Define a class `Shape` with a `std::string` member `m_name`, a constructor taking `const std::string&`, a public `const std::string& getName() const` accessor, and a public `std::string describe() const` that returns `"<name>: generic shape"`.
- Define a class `Circle` that publicly inherits from `Shape`. It has an additional `double m_radius` member. Its constructor takes `const std::string&` and `double`. It has its own `std::string describe() const` that returns `"<name> r=<radius>"` (use `std::to_string` for the radius).
- Define a free function `void report(const Shape& s)` that prints `Report: ` followed by the result of calling `describe()` on `s`.
- In `main`, read the name and radius, create a `Circle`, print `Circle: ` followed by calling `describe()` directly on the circle, then call `report(circle)`, then print `Direct: ` followed by calling `describe()` directly on the circle again.

test_cases.label: Disk with radius 5
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Circle: Disk r=5.000000
Report: Disk: generic shape
Direct: Disk r=5.000000

test_cases.label: Ring with radius 2.5
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Circle: Ring r=2.500000
Report: Ring: generic shape
Direct: Ring r=2.500000

test_cases.label: Wheel with radius 10
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Circle: Wheel r=10.000000
Report: Wheel: generic shape
Direct: Wheel r=10.000000
